text: In this presentation, we will discuss the surgical technique for a patient undergoing a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy. The patient is a forty-two-year-old female who has struggled with obesity for most of her adult life. Her preoperative BMI was thirty-seven point eight and her comorbidities include hypertension and obstructive sleep apnea requiring CPAP. Her preoperative EGD revealed chronic gastritis with H. pylori for which she completed treatment prior to her operation.

The patient is positioned supine with arms outstretched. An orogastric tube is inserted post-intubation. A small stab incision is made in the left subcostal region for the insertion of a varus needle, followed by insufflation to fifteen millimeters of mercury. A twelve millimeter port is placed using an optical trocar. Additional ports are placed under direct visualization: a twelve millimeter left peri-umbilical port for the laparoscope, a five millimeter right subcostal trocar below the liver edge, and a fifteen millimeter port in the right hemiabdomen. A bilateral tap block is performed in four quadrants using diluted liposomal bupivacaine for postoperative pain control.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and a Nathanson liver retractor is inserted through a subxiphoid stab incision, avoiding direct pressure between liver lobes to prevent tearing. Insufflation pressure is reduced to eight for optimal working space. Dissection begins along the inferior aspect of the stomach's greater curvature, using a grasper to hold the stomach under tension while the assistant retracts the greater curvature fat and vessels. A bipolar sealing energy device is used to dissect along the stomach edge towards the fundus, dividing the gastroepiploic vessels while preserving blood supply to the omentum and spleen.

The surgeon approaches the spleen and uses a double burn technique to ensure hemostasis of the short gastric vessels, followed by triggering the cut function medially. Posterior attachments are carefully dissected to avoid dividing branches of the left gastric artery. Attention is then turned to the inferior greater curvature attachments, working towards the pylorus, with dissection performed from the assistant's side of the table. Care is taken to avoid deep dissection that could enter the transverse colon mesentery and infracolic space, potentially creating an internal hernia site. The dissection is carried close to the pylorus to ensure the distal stomach is free from adhesions for the first staple fire.

The surgeon retracts the fundus anterior medially in a spiral motion to expose the area for further dissection. The superior splenic pole vessels, including the posterior short gastric artery, are coagulated and divided. The dissection continues with the phrenoesophageal capsule, separating the angle of His fibers from the left crura. The inferior phrenic artery is exposed, and any significant arterial branches feeding the gastroesophageal junction are preserved to minimize ischemia risk.

The surgeon begins the gastric stapling part of the sleeve gastrectomy at the junction between the antrum and the body of the stomach, approximately three to six centimeters from the pylorus. The left-handed grasper stabilizes the stomach, and a powered stapler device is positioned flat and even with the stomach. The stapler is articulated to the right to prevent narrowing at the incisura, partially closed to grasp the stomach, adjusted for proper positioning, fully closed, and fired.

The surgeon inserts a forty French bougie into the stomach along the lesser curvature, positioning it next to the pylorus. The second staple fire is performed with the stapler articulated to hug the bougie, causing the stomach to slightly dimple. The left hand grasper stabilizes the stomach by grasping the tail of the previous staple line, while the assistant gently retracts the greater curvature to ensure the posterior stomach is included in the staple fire.

The surgeon removes migratory crotch stitches to prevent bunching at the staple line corners. Black staple cartridges are used initially for the thicker antrum, transitioning to purple cartridges for the thinner tissue of the stomach body and fundus. Reinforcement buttress material is applied to all staple lines. The stapler is aimed towards the inferior phrenic artery near the GE junction to avoid disrupting the esophageal wall or leaving a dog ear. The lateral and posterior stomach are retracted to prevent folding at the staple site. After closure, the stapler tips are checked to ensure no tissue attachment. The staple line is inspected and cut with laparoscopic shears to separate the stomach specimen from the gastric sleeve.

The endoscope is inserted into the gastric sleeve and guided to the pylorus. As the scope is withdrawn, the staple line is inspected for integrity and hemostasis, while the assistant irrigates the stomach to confirm a negative leak test. The staple line appears straight, resembling a well. The scope is reinserted to the antrum, and insufflation is evacuated. The liver retractor is removed, and a suture is used to secure the end of the gastric specimen, which is then pulled into the trocar and removed. The fascia is stretched with a clamp to facilitate specimen removal, and the initial skin incision is extended if necessary. The stomach is extracted by alternating retraction on the staple line and greater curvature sides. The specimen retraction site is closed using a fascial closure device.
